perturbation.original_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, disclose to anyone, or use or otherwise exploit for its own benefit or for the benefit of anyone other than Purchaser, any Confidential Information, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Seller may make disclosure of Confidential Information if Seller reasonably concludes that public disclosure of Confidential Information is required by applicable legal requirements and Seller (i) gives Purchaser written notice of such proposed disclosure as far in advance of such disclosure as is reasonably practicable, (ii) cooperates reasonably with Purchaser in its efforts to protect the information from disclosure, including, without limitation, assisting Purchaser in obtaining, at Purchaser’s expense, an appropriate protective order or other reliable assurance that confidential treatment will be accorded such information, and (iii) limits its disclosure to the minimum required by applicable legal requirements unless Purchaser agrees in writing to a greater level of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, it will not, directly or indirectly, disclose to anyone, or use or otherwise exploit for its own benefit or for the benefit of anyone other than Purchaser, any Confidential Information, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement. Seller may make disclosure of Confidential Information if Seller reasonably concludes that public disclosure of Confidential Information is required by applicable legal requirements.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the conditions requiring advance notice, cooperation with efforts to protect information, and limiting disclosure, the modified text weakens the protection afforded to confidential information in the event of a legally required disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act, 18 U.S. Code § 1839
perturbation.law_explanation: The Defend Trade Secrets Act provides protection for trade secrets, but it also contemplates procedures for legally compelled disclosures. The original text acknowledged the seller's obligation to protect the confidentiality of the trade secret information even if legally compelled to disclose. Removing the conditions weakens this obligation, potentially violating the spirit of protecting trade secrets.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The original contract text outlines specific actions the seller must take to protect confidential information, even when disclosure is legally required. These include providing advance notice, cooperating with the purchaser to protect the information, and limiting the disclosure to the minimum required. The changed text removes these conditions, weakening the protection of the confidential information. Although snippet 2 defines terms, the absence of the specific language creates a scenario where the Defend Trade Secrets Act, as explained by the LLM, is undermined because the seller is no longer obligated to take active steps to protect the information during legally compelled disclosures, contradicting the spirit of protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, Seller will not, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or influence any customer, supplier, lender, lessor or any other Person that has a business relationship with the Business in the Protected Market, or which had on the date of this Agreement a business relationship with the Business in the Protected Market, to discontinue or reduce the extent of such relationship with the Business in the Protected Market; it being understood that, nothing herein shall restrict Seller from carrying on or transacting business with any such Person in respect of any Seller Exclusive Products.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller hereby agrees that, during the Restricted Period, except as permitted by Section 5 of this Agreement, Seller will not, directly or indirectly, solicit, induce or influence any customer, supplier, lender, lessor or any other Person that has a business relationship with the Business in the Protected Market, or which had on the date of this Agreement a business relationship with the Business in the Protected Market, to discontinue or reduce the extent of such relationship with the Business in the Protected Market.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'understanding' that the seller *can* transact business with the entities as long as it regards 'seller exclusive products' creates ambiguity in the contract by limiting the seller's ability to continue to interact with its clients regarding those exclusive products.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a direct violation, overly broad non-solicitation clauses can be viewed as restraints of trade if they unduly limit competition. By removing the clarification regarding Seller Exclusive Products, the clause becomes potentially broader and could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade, especially if Seller's exclusive products are significantly related to the market.

Trusts, etc., in restraint of trade illegal; penalty Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations, is declared to be illegal. Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by removing the exception for 'Seller Exclusive Products,' broadens the scope of the non-solicitation clause. This potentially creates an unreasonable restraint of trade, as the seller is now limited in interacting with customers even regarding their own exclusive products. This contradicts the Sherman Act, which prohibits 'Every contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations,' as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2. The original text contained an exception that allowed some level of competition, which is removed in the modified version. Removing this exception creates a structural flaw as the original text had clear exceptions, which now do not exist. This creates ambiguity in the contract by limiting the seller's ability to continue to interact with its clients regarding those exclusive products.
